serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Risotto al Pesto is approximately 350 g, presented as a single plate of creamy pesto-dressed Carnaroli rice.

nutrient_source.title: Risotto, with pesto sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Risotto al Pesto as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked Carnaroli/Arborio rice (FDC ID 169704, cooked white rice) with USDA SR Legacy data for basil pesto sauce (FDC ID 172944, pesto sauce, commercially prepared), weighted at approximately 75% cooked rice and 25% pesto sauce by mass, consistent with standard restaurant-style risotto al pesto proportions. Parmesan and butter contributions are folded into the pesto fraction. All component values are from USDA FoodData Central approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (Arborio/Carnaroli type, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Risotto al Pesto as a composite dish. The GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Arborio/Carnaroli-type white rice cooked as risotto (GI approximately 54–69 range; conservative lower-bound value of 54 used). The addition of pesto sauce (fat and protein) further attenuates the glycemic response, supporting use of the lower bound. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 73.5 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 39.7. GI ≤ 55 → gi_category = low per Veritas threshold rules.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1085 mg per restaurant serving of 350 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a restaurant serving and request reduced-salt stock preparation, as a full 350 g serving contributes approximately 47% of the 2300 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~40) despite a low-to-moderate GI (~54), driven by the large carbohydrate mass in a restaurant portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–180 g) to keep the glycemic load below 20, and pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side to further blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g from Parmesan and butter) and elevated sodium per restaurant serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Risotto al Pesto to an occasional meal, request preparation with reduced butter and Parmesan, and balance the meal with unsaturated-fat-rich foods given the olive oil and pine nut content of pesto.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Risotto al Pesto is a classic Northern Italian rice dish made with Carnaroli rice — a high-starch, short-grain variety prized for its ability to absorb stock while releasing starch to create a naturally creamy texture — dressed with Genovese-style basil pesto. The pesto sauce is traditionally prepared from fresh basil leaves, pine nuts, Parmesan cheese, garlic, and extra-virgin olive oil. The risotto is cooked using the classic absorption method: onion and garlic are softened in butter or olive oil, the dry rice is toasted briefly, white wine is added and absorbed, then warm stock is ladled in gradually with constant stirring until the rice is al dente and the mixture is creamy (all'onda). Pesto is stirred in off the heat to preserve its vibrant green colour and aromatic volatile compounds. The finished dish is rich, herbaceous, and savoury, with a creamy mouthfeel from the starch and cheese, a bright basil fragrance, and a mild nuttiness from the pine nuts. Nutritionally, Risotto al Pesto is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful carbohydrate load from the rice, moderate fat from olive oil, pine nuts, and dairy, and modest protein. The sodium content is clinically relevant for hypertension management. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 54 (low category), but the glycemic load per restaurant serving is high (~40), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan or halal due to Parmesan (animal rennet) and possible wine use. It is commonly served as a primo piatto (first course) in Italian cuisine and appears on Italian restaurant menus in Singapore, including at Ricciotti.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pesto risotto?
Is pesto risotto suitable for diabetics?
What rice is used in risotto al pesto?
How much sodium is in pesto risotto?
What is the glycemic index of risotto?
Can I eat risotto al pesto if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Risotto al Pesto as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked white rice and commercially prepared pesto sauce, weighted by standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Arborio/Carnaroli-type risotto rice. Confidence is medium rather than low because both component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates from USDA FoodData Central (cooked white rice FDC 169704 and pesto sauce FDC 172944); no exact composite entry exists in any approved primary source for Risotto al Pesto. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this dish. 3. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Arborio/Carnaroli-type risotto rice; the fat and protein content of pesto sauce is expected to attenuate the glycemic response, supporting the lower-bound estimate. GI ≤ 55 → gi_category set to 'low' per Veritas threshold rules. 4. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical Italian restaurant portion norms; the actual Ricciotti restaurant portion may differ. 5. Halal status is uncertain due to possible use of wine in cooking and Parmesan with animal rennet; is_halal set to false by default. 6. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and should be treated as approximate. 7. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. No mandatory non-English local names are required for Italian cuisine under Veritas v2 rules.
